one_line_takeaway: A realistic, tool-calling indirect-prompt-injection competition yields a 208k-prompt adaptive-attack dataset showing that end-to-end injection is hard (<1% success) and that stacking detection defenses (LLM Judge + updated TaskTracker) can reduce successful attacks to near zero.
key_finding: End-to-end attacks are hard and layered defenses help substantially. In Phase 1 only 3,018 of 370,724 submissions (0.8%) were end-to-end successful; in Phase 2 only 306 of 90,916 (0.3%). The multi-stage funnel for Phase 1 shows 371k submissions -> 362k retrieved -> 180k undetected -> 7.2k called send_email -> 6.1k correct destination -> 3k correct content (<1% of total). Among detection defenses, LLM Judge was strongest (lowest attacker TSR, highest recall 0.994 Phase 1) but most compute-heavy; Prompt Shield was weakest (recall 0.604 Phase 1, highest attacker TSR). TaskTracker improved markedly with newer backbone models (recall 0.761 with Phi-3 14B up to 0.949 with Phi-3.5 MoE in Phase 1, and up to 0.998 with Phi-4 14B in Phase 2). Stacking all defenses drove Level-4 GPT-4 TSR down to 0.166 and, notably, using all defenses combined with GPT-4o in Phase 2 yielded zero successful attacks. There is a detection/success trade-off: detected attacks called the tool more often (34k of 183k detected) than undetected ones, i.e., the most aggressive prompts are also the most detectable. Winning attackers exploited special chat tokens (<|user|>, <|assistant|>, <|end|>), multilingual/obfuscated text, HTML-like formatting, fake-email + synthetic-conversation blocks, and many succeeded with 'declarative-sentence' style injections rather than explicit instructions.
core_contribution: The paper releases LLMail-Inject, a large-scale dataset and analysis from a public adaptive indirect-prompt-injection competition run as an official IEEE SaTML 2025 track. The authors' own contribution is (1) the end-to-end challenge infrastructure simulating an LLM-based email assistant (retriever + LLM + send_email tool + stacked defenses), (2) the released dataset of 208,095 unique attack prompts (plus raw submissions and LLM-annotator labels), and (3) a quantitative analysis comparing how deployed defenses (Spotlighting, Prompt Shield, LLM Judge, TaskTracker) hold up against adaptive attackers who knew which defense was active. Distinct from the Related Work (Gandalf/Lakera, HackAPrompt, and the 2024 SaTML CTF), the authors position LLMail-Inject as the first challenge to study indirect prompt injection in a realistic setting where the LLM can actually call tools and where the attack must succeed end-to-end (be retrieved, evade detection, invoke the tool with correct arguments, and in Level 4 exfiltrate a secret figure).
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against an LLM-integrated email assistant. The attacker sends a single email (subject + body) into a victim's mailbox. When the victim asks the assistant to summarize or answer a query, the retriever surfaces the attacker's email into the LLM context. The attacker's goal is to make the assistant invoke the send_email tool without user request, with destination=contact@contact.com and content=confirmation (Levels 1-3); in Level 4 the attacker must also exfiltrate a secret Q2 profit-forecast figure from a separate email into the content field. The attacker cannot see the LLM output and does not know the randomly suffixed tool name. Attackers were told which defenses were active, making attacks adaptive. Defenses evaluated: Spotlighting (preventive delimiter/marking), Prompt Shield (proprietary black-box classifier), LLM Judge (LLM-as-guardrail), TaskTracker (linear probe on activation deltas / task drift), and a stacked combination.
limitations: Authors note: Phi-3 was non-deterministic (attributed to API limits) and had degraded utility under spotlighting, so that specific setup was excluded from analysis and from Phase 2. Large subsets of the dataset lack ground-truth labels and rely on an LLM-annotator (104,583 injection / 9,452 not-injection / 23,911 unclear in Phase 1), and the unclear/not-injection sets may still contain attacks; the authors therefore advise against directly training on the dataset. The attack objective is deliberately narrow (one tool call with fixed arguments), limiting objective diversity even though attack styles are varied. Reviewer-side: results are specific to two LLMs and the particular defense implementations/thresholds (tuned to <5% FPR), so absolute rates should not be read as universal; classifier recall numbers were computed post-hoc at high thresholds (0.99) chosen for 0% FPR on synthetic emails, which may not reflect deployment operating points.

datasets_used: LLMail-Inject challenge dataset (this paper's own release): 208,095 unique attack prompts (169,598 Phase 1 + 38,497 Phase 2), 461,640 raw submissions (370,724 Phase 1 + 90,916 Phase 2)
Synthetic benign emails authored per level (e.g., Egyptology persona) used as mailbox context and for false-positive testing
TaskTracker training data and a proprietary Prompt Shield dataset (from Abdelnabi et al. 2025) used to train/threshold the detection defenses
quantitative_results: Phase 1 funnel: 362k retrieved, 180k undetected, 7.2k tool-called, 6.1k correct destination, 3k correct content (<1%)
Detected-attack funnel Phase 1: 183k detected -> 34k tool-called -> 28.6k correct destination -> 21.4k correct content
Table 1 Team Success Rate (Level 4, GPT-4): Prompt Shield 0.600, Spotlight 0.529, TaskTracker 0.440, LLM Judge 0.318, All defenses 0.166
Table 2 avg trials before first success (Phase 1 average): Prompt Shield 88.9, TaskTracker 159.3, LLM Judge 506.2

Table 3 detection recall Phase 1 / Phase 2: Prompt Shield v1 0.604/0.425, Prompt Shield v2 0.678/0.487, TaskTracker Phi-3 14B 0.761/0.896, TaskTracker Phi-3.5 MoE 0.949/0.991, TaskTracker Phi-4 14B 0.881/0.998, LLM Judge 0.994/0.965
Table 4 ensemble recall (Phase 1): combinations of Prompt Shield / TaskTracker Phi-3 / TaskTracker Phi-4 / LLM Judge range from ~0.89 up to 0.998
Phase 2: all defenses combined with GPT-4o produced zero successful attacks
Annotations Phase 1: 25,323 tool-triggering (ground-truth injections); LLM-annotator: 104,583 injection, 9,452 not-injection, 23,911 unclear
Participation: 402 teams / 839 registered participants (292 teams / 621 participants Phase 1; 110 teams / 218 participants Phase 2)
baselines_compared: Spotlighting (preventive delimiter/data-marking defense, Hines et al. 2024)
Prompt Shield (proprietary black-box prompt-injection classifier, Microsoft 2024; v1 and v2)
TaskTracker (activation-delta / task-drift linear probe, Abdelnabi et al. 2025; Phi-3 14B, Phi-3.5 MoE, Phi-4 14B backbones)
LLM backbones compared: microsoft/Phi-3-medium-128k-instruct vs GPT-4o-mini (with instruction hierarchy)
